title: uni

uni

uni

Connector option that defines the default Unicode encoding used by the TXT connector. The encoding for a specific entity can be specified using the /uni switch in an entity-to-connector assignment.

Default Unicode encoding:

[DRIVER_SETTINGS] USYS$TXT_PARAMS uni = utf8 | utf16 | utf16le | utf16be | utf32 | utf32le | utf32be

Entity-specific Unicode encoding:

[ENTITIES] Entity $TXT: FileName /append /uni = utf8 | utf16 | utf16le | utf16be | utf32 | utf32le | utf32be

Values

Unicode comes in various encodings, such as UTF8, UTF16, and UTF32, which determine the number of bytes used to represent a character. The le and be suffixes refer to little endian and big endian byte order.

For UTF16 and UTF32, these encodings are in the native endian-ness of the machine. This means that a file written in UTF32 on a little-endian machine is actually written in UTF32LE. On a big-endian machine, it will not be seen as UTF32 because that is interpreted as UTF32BE and will require a conversion. For more information, see Data Conversion.

Description

Use the uni connector option to specify the default Unicode encoding used by the TXT connector. If this setting is not present, the default encoding specified by $DEF_CHARSET is used.

The encoding for a specific entity can be specified using the /uni switch in an entity-to-connector assignment. If the /uni switch is used, it overrides the encoding defaults specified in USYS$TXT_PARAMS.

Byte Order Mark (BOM)

The encoding of a Unicode file is defined by the byte order mark (BOM) and all of the data must be in that encoding. This has the following implications for reading and writing text files using the TXT connector:

  • If an entity specifies a file that already exists, the BOM in that file will be read and will override any encoding specification in the assignment file.
  • If no BOM is present and the assignment file specifies Unicode encoding, an error will be returned.
  • If the file does not exist and a Unicode encoding is specified, a BOM will be written.
  • If a second entity appends data to the same files but has a different Unicode encoding specified, it will be ignored and the encoding of the creator of the file will be used.
  • If the entity contains diverse fields that cannot all be converted to the desired Unicode encoding, then no BOM will be written.
Note: By setting the bom connector option (or /bom switch for an entity), you can define whether or not the TXT driver writes or requires a BOM to be present in Unicode files. For more information, see bom.

Example: Configuration for Unicode

The following example shows how you can configure the TXT connector for Unicode.

[SETTINGS]
$DEF_CHARSET=utf8           1
[DRIVER_SETTINGS]
TXT = U2.0 
USYS$TXT_PARAMS = uni=utf16         2
[PATHS]
$TXT = TXT 
[ENTITIES]
Entity1.cs   $TXT:Entity1.fdd/append/uni=utf16     3
Entity2.cs   $TXT:Entity2.fdd/append/uni=utf32be
  1. Set the default character for storing data in String fields with C packing code to Unicode UTF8.
  2. Set the default encoding for the TXT connector to UTF16.
  3. Override the default encoding for specific entities that the TXT connector handles.
